title: Merge Sort
code: private static int[] Merge(int[] left, int[] right)

frames.caption: Sort nums = [38, 27, 43, 3] by divide and conquer: split in half, sort each half, merge.
frames.note: Merge sort is the canonical divide-and-conquer algorithm: DIVIDE the array into two halves, CONQUER by sorting each half recursively (trusting the recursion to handle the smaller problem), then COMBINE the two sorted halves with a linear merge. The recurrence is T(n) = 2·T(n/2) + O(n), which solves to O(n log n) by the master theorem.

frames.caption: Divide [38, 27, 43, 3] at mid=2: left = [38, 27], right = [43, 3].
frames.note: DIVIDE: merge sort splits the range exactly in half at mid = lo + (hi - lo)/2 — a trivial O(1) step (just index arithmetic) — then recurses on both halves. Splitting in the exact middle every time is what guarantees log n levels of recursion regardless of the input, which is why merge sort is O(n log n) in the best, average, AND worst case, with no degenerate input like quicksort has.

frames.caption: Divide [38, 27] at mid=1: left = [38], right = [27].
frames.note: DIVIDE: merge sort splits the range exactly in half at mid = lo + (hi - lo)/2 — a trivial O(1) step (just index arithmetic) — then recurses on both halves. Splitting in the exact middle every time is what guarantees log n levels of recursion regardless of the input, which is why merge sort is O(n log n) in the best, average, AND worst case, with no degenerate input like quicksort has.

frames.caption: Base case: [38] has 1 element — already sorted, return as is.
frames.note: BASE CASE: a subarray of length 0 or 1 is already sorted, so the recursion bottoms out and returns immediately with no work. Every recursive path must reach a base case that shrinks the input; here each call halves the range, so we are guaranteed to terminate at these singletons.

frames.caption: Base case: [27] has 1 element — already sorted, return as is.
frames.note: BASE CASE: a subarray of length 0 or 1 is already sorted, so the recursion bottoms out and returns immediately with no work. Every recursive path must reach a base case that shrinks the input; here each call halves the range, so we are guaranteed to terminate at these singletons.

frames.caption: Combine: merge sorted left [38] and right [27] into a[0..1].
frames.note: The two halves are now individually sorted (the recursive leap of faith: trust the calls returned correct smaller answers). All that remains is to interleave them into one sorted run — the merge step does this in a single linear pass.

frames.caption: Compare left front 38 vs right front 27: 38 > 27, take 27 from the right -> a[0].
frames.note: COMBINE (merge step): walk the two sorted halves with two pointers (i over the left run, j over the right) and always copy the smaller front element forward, advancing only that pointer. Each comparison places exactly one element, so merging two runs of total length m is O(m) — and because every one of the log n levels does O(n) total merge work, the whole sort is O(n log n). The tie-break uses left <= right, which keeps the sort STABLE: equal keys from the left half are emitted first, preserving their original order.

frames.caption: Right run drained — copy remaining left 38 -> a[1].
frames.note: One run is exhausted, so the remaining tail of the other run is already sorted and larger than everything placed so far — copy it over verbatim with no more comparisons. This drain phase is why the merge never needs to look back: the two-pointer invariant guarantees what is left is in final order.

frames.caption: Merged a[0..1] = [27, 38] — one sorted run.
frames.note: This range is now a single sorted run, written back in place. The merge is the reusable primitive at the heart of merge sort — the very same two-pointer combine powers LC 21 (Merge Two Sorted Lists), external/k-way merges for data too big for RAM, and LINQ OrderBy. Master it once and you have the COMBINE step for the whole divide-and-conquer sort.

frames.caption: Divide [43, 3] at mid=3: left = [43], right = [3].
frames.note: DIVIDE: merge sort splits the range exactly in half at mid = lo + (hi - lo)/2 — a trivial O(1) step (just index arithmetic) — then recurses on both halves. Splitting in the exact middle every time is what guarantees log n levels of recursion regardless of the input, which is why merge sort is O(n log n) in the best, average, AND worst case, with no degenerate input like quicksort has.

frames.caption: Base case: [43] has 1 element — already sorted, return as is.
frames.note: BASE CASE: a subarray of length 0 or 1 is already sorted, so the recursion bottoms out and returns immediately with no work. Every recursive path must reach a base case that shrinks the input; here each call halves the range, so we are guaranteed to terminate at these singletons.

frames.caption: Base case: [3] has 1 element — already sorted, return as is.
frames.note: BASE CASE: a subarray of length 0 or 1 is already sorted, so the recursion bottoms out and returns immediately with no work. Every recursive path must reach a base case that shrinks the input; here each call halves the range, so we are guaranteed to terminate at these singletons.

frames.note: The two halves are now individually sorted (the recursive leap of faith: trust the calls returned correct smaller answers). All that remains is to interleave them into one sorted run — the merge step does this in a single linear pass.

frames.caption: Compare left front 43 vs right front 3: 43 > 3, take 3 from the right -> a[2].

frames.caption: Right run drained — copy remaining left 43 -> a[3].

This drain phase is why the merge never needs to look back: the two-pointer invariant guarantees what is left is in final order.

frames.caption: Merged a[2..3] = [3, 43] — one sorted run.
frames.note: This range is now a single sorted run, written back in place. The merge is the reusable primitive at the heart of merge sort — the very same two-pointer combine powers LC 21 (Merge Two Sorted Lists), external/k-way merges for data too big for RAM, and LINQ OrderBy. Master it once and you have the COMBINE step for the whole divide-and-conquer sort.

frames.caption: Combine: merge sorted left [27, 38] and right [3, 43] into a[0..3].
frames.note: The two halves are now individually sorted (the recursive leap of faith: trust the calls returned correct smaller answers). All that remains is to interleave them into one sorted run — the merge step does this in a single linear pass.

frames.caption: Compare left front 27 vs right front 3: 27 > 3, take 3 from the right -> a[0].
frames.note: COMBINE (merge step): walk the two sorted halves with two pointers (i over the left run, j over the right) and always copy the smaller front element forward, advancing only that pointer. Each comparison places exactly one element, so merging two runs of total length m is O(m) — and because every one of the log n levels does O(n) total merge work, the whole sort is O(n log n). The tie-break uses left <= right, which keeps the sort STABLE: equal keys from the left half are emitted first, preserving their original order.

frames.caption: Compare left front 27 vs right front 43: 27 <= 43, take 27 from the left -> a[1].
frames.note: COMBINE (merge step): walk the two sorted halves with two pointers (i over the left run, j over the right) and always copy the smaller front element forward, advancing only that pointer. Each comparison places exactly one element, so merging two runs of total length m is O(m) — and because every one of the log n levels does O(n) total merge work, the whole sort is O(n log n). The tie-break uses left <= right, which keeps the sort STABLE: equal keys from the left half are emitted first, preserving their original order.

frames.caption: Compare left front 38 vs right front 43: 38 <= 43, take 38 from the left -> a[2].
frames.note: COMBINE (merge step): walk the two sorted halves with two pointers (i over the left run, j over the right) and always copy the smaller front element forward, advancing only that pointer. Each comparison places exactly one element, so merging two runs of total length m is O(m) — and because every one of the log n levels does O(n) total merge work, the whole sort is O(n log n). The tie-break uses left <= right, which keeps the sort STABLE: equal keys from the left half are emitted first, preserving their original order.

frames.caption: Left run drained — copy remaining right 43 -> a[3].
frames.note: One run is exhausted, so the remaining tail of the other run is already sorted and larger than everything placed so far — copy it over verbatim with no more comparisons. This drain phase is why the merge never needs to look back: the two-pointer invariant guarantees what is left is in final order.

frames.caption: Merged a[0..3] = [3, 27, 38, 43] — one sorted run.
frames.note: This range is now a single sorted run, written back in place. The merge is the reusable primitive at the heart of merge sort — the very same two-pointer combine powers LC 21 (Merge Two Sorted Lists), external/k-way merges for data too big for RAM, and LINQ OrderBy. Master it once and you have the COMBINE step for the whole divide-and-conquer sort.

frames.caption: Sorted: [3, 27, 38, 43]. Merge sort is O(n log n) in all cases, stable, and not in-place.
frames.note: The top-level merge produced the fully sorted array. Recap the headline facts: O(n log n) in every case (log n levels × O(n) merge each), STABLE (the left<=right tie-break preserves equal keys' order), but NOT in-place — it needs O(n) auxiliary space for the merge buffers (plus O(log n) recursion stack). That guaranteed worst case is merge sort's edge over quicksort.
